decisions.message: I have completed 1 task and got a rating of 4, but its says task limit reached, can i get unthrottled now?
decisions.reason: This question is about account policy, throttling, and task limits - topics that require sensitive account management decisions and are not covered in the provided project memory or guidelines. The user should contact the project team directly.
guidelineSnippets:
Task Overview
Description
Onboarding &
Installations
Before tasking for the first time, you must complete the project
onboarding and reach out to the project team after completing your
quality assessment. Then you will set up Cursor and Docker. You
only need to do this once!
Setup and
Analysis
First, you will clone the assigned repo and set up your environment.
You will also explore the codebase so that you understand its
structure and functionality.
Issue Creation
and Solution
You will write a prompt that asks the agent to make changes to the
codebase based on an assigned issue (e.g., bug fixes or new
features). You will make sure that your prompt is challenging
enough that at least one model fails to solve it. Then, you will write
your own Golden Solution to your prompt.
Testing
You will create unit tests that can check whether a given solution
adds all of the requested functionality without breaking any
previously existing functionality.
Docker and
Scripts
You will prepare Dockerfiles and scripts that ensure that your work
on the task is reproducible.
Validation Script You will run a validation script that checks that your solution passes
all of the unit tests that you wrote.
Add Tags You will add relevant tags to your task to categorize it.
Rubric Creation You will write a rubric that consists of a series of criteria that can
grade how good or bad a response to your prompt is.
Evaluation Script
Before submission, you will run an evaluation script on your task.
The suite checks the quality and alignment of your prompt, tests,
rubrics, gold patch, Dockerfiles, and granularity.
Step 0 Onboarding & Installations (return to TOC
Project Onboarding (return to TOC
Before your first task, you will be guided through our onboarding process, where you will
learn the basics of what you need to know to succeed on the project.
3

As part of the onboarding process, you will submit a quality assessment.
Please reach out to the project team in the Zoom War Room after completing the quality
assessment. This step is mandatory for you to begin tasking. The project team will need
to perform a manual review to ensure that you passed the quality assessment and to
provide access to Cursor.
Environment Setup (return to TOC
Before getting started with the project, both Cursor and Docker must be properly set up
and running. Here is a quick guide to make sure your environment is properly set up.
Cursor
● Follow this link and download the 2.6 cursor version. If you already have Cursor
installed, uninstall it and install this specific version, without this version, the models
wonʼt work.
● Even if you already have access to the Cursor Team Plan, reach out to the Zoom
War Room and ask any of the QMs for a new plan, as it must be renewed for this
project.
● Go to the Key Manager: https://cursor-intelligence-service.outlier.ai/key-manager
● Click on View API Key
● Open Cursor Settings → Models
● Disable all models, and add:
○ claude-qwen3527b-scale
○ claude-4.6-sonnet-scale
● Enable OpenAI API Key; paste in your API Key and Override Base URL with
https://cursor-intelligence-api.outlier.ai/api/v1
● Click "Verify".
● Test these models in Cursor now.
Docker
● Make sure Docker is installed and up to date. If you don't have it yet, follow this
guide.
● Ensure Docker has enough RAM and disk space to run containers smoothly.
● Clean up unused images and containers regularly to free up space and avoid build
errors.
Project Structure (return to TOC
4

If itʼs not bug injection, please upload an empty basetoinstance.patch file to the task
Please note that the Outlier platform does not allow literally empty files. Adding an empty
line or a space is acceptable)
2.4 Test the Issue Message (return to TOC
Make sure the issue message is challenging enough to cause at least one model to fail.
To do so, open only the App/ folder in Cursor and have both models available: Sonnet 4.6
and Qwen 3.5 27b, if you donʼt have them available go to the Environment Setup section
in the appendix to see a full guide on how to set them up.
● If it is a Bug injection task, make sure the app/ folder contains the bug
● Paste the issue message and select one model (Sonnet 4.6 or Qwen 3.5 27b). Then
check whether it fails to create a valid solution.
● Repeat the same with the other model.
● If both models solve the issue correctly, rewrite or adjust the issue message to
increase its complexity or change the issue topic until one of them fails.
● Ensure that the reported failure is directly tied to a specific requirement described in
the issue message. The model should only fail when it does not correctly implement
an expected behavior explicitly requested in the prompt, not because of
assumptions, inferred expectations, or invented edge cases that are not part of the
original issue description. If you want to know about valid failures refer to the
Sample Invalid Model Failures section.
● When at least one model fails, and the issue message meets all requirements, select
"Yes" and continue with the task.
● Extract the model failure trace from Cursor as documentation. If both models fail,
capturing one of them is enough). If you need a guide on how to generate that trace,
go to the How to generate the model trace section.
● Generate a model_trace.patch containing the exact changes made by the model
during its trace. If you need a guide on how to generate that file, go to the How to
generate model_trace.patch section.
IMPORTANT Ensure that only the App/ folder is opened in Cursor so the
models do not have access to the patches with the solution (you can open a
new Cursor window with just the App/ content).

● App
● dockerfiles
● scripts
● patches
1.2 Clone Repo & Analyze Codebase (return to TOC
First, open a new terminal and clone the given task repo using the provided repository
clone command. Rename the cloned folder as App/. After that, explore the codebase you
cloned:
● Review the code structure and architecture
● Understand dependencies and build process
● Understand the issue type you were assigned for this task Review Issue Types
Examples section in the Appendix for specific examples):
○ New feature request: Adding a new functionality not currently in the given
code.
○ Bug injection + resolution: Introducing a breaking change to working code
○ Migration: Adapting all or part of the existing code to a new
library/pattern/version without losing its original functionality.
○ Performance Optimization: Improve speed, efficiency, or resource usage with
no changes to the original functionality.
○ Maintenance or testing enhancement: Updating dependencies, tests, or tooling
Step 2 Issue Creation and Solution (return to TOC
2.1 Write Issue Statement (return to TOC
After reviewing the codebase, you will create a prompt based on the issue type that you
were assigned. The prompt should make a request to the agent based on your issue type.
It should also be complex enough that it challenges the models (Sonnet 4.6 and Qwen
3.5 27b) so that one of them fails. You can see examples of the different issue types in
Issue Types Examples section in the Appendix.
Prompt Guidelines:
● Specific enough that it is possible for the model to succeed
● Not so detailed that it gives away the solution to the model

1.2 Clone Repo & Analyze Codebase (return to TOC
First, open a new terminal and clone the given task repo using the provided repository
clone command. Rename the cloned folder as App/. After that, explore the codebase you
cloned:
● Review the code structure and architecture
● Understand dependencies and build process
● Understand the issue type you were assigned for this task Review Issue Types
Examples section in the Appendix for specific examples):
○ New feature request: Adding a new functionality not currently in the given
code.
○ Bug injection + resolution: Introducing a breaking change to working code
○ Migration: Adapting all or part of the existing code to a new
library/pattern/version without losing its original functionality.
○ Performance Optimization: Improve speed, efficiency, or resource usage with
no changes to the original functionality.
○ Maintenance or testing enhancement: Updating dependencies, tests, or tooling
Step 2 Issue Creation and Solution (return to TOC
2.1 Write Issue Statement (return to TOC
After reviewing the codebase, you will create a prompt based on the issue type that you
were assigned. The prompt should make a request to the agent based on your issue type.
It should also be complex enough that it challenges the models (Sonnet 4.6 and Qwen
3.5 27b) so that one of them fails. You can see examples of the different issue types in
Issue Types Examples section in the Appendix.
Prompt Guidelines:
● Specific enough that it is possible for the model to succeed
● Not so detailed that it gives away the solution to the model
